card.title: There Are Different Sizes of Infinity
card.teaser: Some infinities are literally larger than others. A 19th-century mathematician went mad proving it.
card.coverImagePrompt: An endless spiral staircase made of numbers, fading into starry darkness above and below, purple cosmic haze, dreamlike cinematic painting

lessons.title: Bigger than endless
lessons.body: For most of human history, infinity was one thing — vague, unreachable, either divine or forbidden. Then in the 1870s, a shy German mathematician named Georg Cantor showed something outrageous: some infinities are strictly larger than others. The backlash cost him his career, and nearly his sanity.

visual.imagePrompt: An old chalkboard covered in overlapping infinity symbols in faint white chalk

lessons.title: How do you compare endless piles?
lessons.body: Cantor's trick: forget counting. Instead, try to pair things up one-to-one. If every item in pile A can be matched with exactly one item in pile B and nothing's left over, the piles are the same size — even if both are infinite. By this rule, there are as many even numbers as integers. Weird, but consistent.

keyTerms.definition: A perfect one-to-one pairing between two sets
The size of a set, even an infinite one

visual.imagePrompt: Two infinite rows of dots connected by arrows pairing them one to one

lessons.title: The diagonal that broke math
lessons.body: Cantor asked: can we pair every whole number with every real number between 0 and 1? He assumed yes, listed them, and then built a new number by changing the first digit of the first entry, the second digit of the second, and so on. The result differed from every number on the list — proving the list was incomplete. The reals are a bigger infinity than the counting numbers. Full stop.

visual.imagePrompt: A handwritten list of decimal numbers with a red diagonal line crossing through key digits

lessons.title: A hierarchy without end
lessons.body: Cantor went further. He proved that the set of all subsets of any infinite set is *itself* a larger infinity — and you can repeat this forever. Infinities of infinities. Most of his peers rejected it as pure nonsense. Henri Poincaré called it a 'grave disease.' Today his diagonal argument is a cornerstone of logic, computer science, and proofs of what computers can never do.
lessons.funFact: Alan Turing adapted Cantor's diagonal trick to prove some problems are uncomputable — the foundation of modern computer science.

visual.imagePrompt: Nested glowing cubes stacking upward into a starry void

lessons.title: Infinity has shape
lessons.body: The lesson isn't just that math hides wonders. It's that some true things are so strange they look wrong. Cantor's infinities broke an intuition humans had held for 2,500 years. He wasn't wrong — we were, and not a little. Next time something feels obviously impossible, ask: obvious to whom?

quiz.question: How did Cantor compare the sizes of infinite sets?
quiz.explanation: If every element of one set pairs perfectly with one element of the other, the sets are the same size.

quiz.question: What did Cantor's diagonal argument prove?
options: Even numbers are fewer than integers
Real numbers are a larger infinity than whole numbers
Zero is a type of infinity
quiz.explanation: By constructing a number different from every entry in any proposed list, Cantor showed the reals can't be listed.

quiz.question: Why is Cantor's diagonal argument still important today?
options: It underpins proofs in computer science about what's computable
quiz.explanation: Turing adapted it to show that some problems, like the halting problem, have no algorithmic solution.

sources.title: Contributions to the Founding of the Theory of Transfinite Numbers
Everything and More: A Compact History of Infinity
sources.publisher: David Foster Wallace, W. W. Norton
